lessons.title: Introduction to inheritance

exercises.title: Basic Inheritance: Vehicle and Car
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Create a class hierarchy where `Car` publicly inherits from `Vehicle`. Demonstrate that the derived class inherits the base class's data and behavior.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `brand` (no spaces, 1-50 characters).
- Line 2: an integer `year`.
- Line 3: an integer `doors`.

## Output

Exactly two lines:
1. `Vehicle: <brand> (<year>)`
2. `Car: <brand> (<year>), <doors> doors`

## Requirements

- Define a class `Vehicle` with public members `std::string brand{}` and `int year{}`.
- `Vehicle` has a public `void printInfo() const` that prints `Vehicle: <brand> (<year>)`.
- Define a class `Car` that publicly inherits from `Vehicle`.
- `Car` adds a public member `int doors{}`.
- `Car` has a public `void printCarInfo() const` that prints `Car: <brand> (<year>), <doors> doors`. Access `brand` and `year` directly (they are inherited).
- In `main`, read `brand`, `year`, and `doors` from stdin. Create a `Car`, assign all three fields, then call `printInfo()` followed by `printCarInfo()`.

exercises.title: Inheritance hierarchy: Person, Student, GradStudent
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a three-level inheritance chain: `GradStudent` inherits from `Student`, which inherits from `Person`. Each level adds one piece of data. Print a summary that uses fields from all three levels, demonstrating that inheritance is transitive.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces, 1-50 characters).
- Line 2: an integer `age`.
- Line 3: an integer `studentId`.
- Line 4: a string `thesis` (no spaces, 1-100 characters, use underscores for spaces).

## Output

Exactly three lines:
1. `Person: <name>, age <age>`
2. `Student: <name>, id <studentId>`
3. `GradStudent: <name>, thesis: <thesis>`

## Requirements

- Define class `Person` with public members `std::string name{}` and `int age{}`. Add a public `void printPerson() const` that prints `Person: <name>, age <age>`.
- Define class `Student` that publicly inherits from `Person`. Add a public member `int studentId{}`. Add a public `void printStudent() const` that prints `Student: <name>, id <studentId>`.
- Define class `GradStudent` that publicly inherits from `Student`. Add a public member `std::string thesis{}`. Add a public `void printGrad() const` that prints `GradStudent: <name>, thesis: <thesis>`.
- In `main`, read all four inputs, create a `GradStudent`, assign all fields, and call `printPerson()`, `printStudent()`, and `printGrad()` in that order.
- Do not use constructors with parameters -- assign each field individually after default construction.

test_cases.label: Alice, age 25, id 1001, thesis on compilers
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person: Alice, age 25
Student: Alice, id 1001
GradStudent: Alice, thesis: Compiler_Optimization

test_cases.label: Bob, age 30, id 2050, thesis on robotics
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person: Bob, age 30
Student: Bob, id 2050
GradStudent: Bob, thesis: Robotics_Navigation

test_cases.label: Zara, age 22, id 9999, thesis on networks
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person: Zara, age 22
Student: Zara, id 9999
GradStudent: Zara, thesis: Network_Security

lessons.title: Basic inheritance in C++

exercises.title: Derive a Student from Person
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Create a base class `Person` and a derived class `Student` that inherits from `Person` using public inheritance. This exercise practices the basic `class Derived : public Base` syntax and calling the base constructor from the derived constructor.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces, 1-50 characters) and an integer `age` (1 <= age <= 120).
- Line 2: an integer `studentId` (1 <= studentId <= 99999).

## Output

- Line 1: `Name: <name>`
- Line 2: `Age: <age>`
- Line 3: `Student ID: <studentId>`

All values come from calling member functions on a single `Student` object.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Person` with private members `std::string m_name` and `int m_age`. Its constructor takes `std::string_view name` and `int age`.
- `Person` has public const getters `getName()` (returns `std::string_view`) and `getAge()` (returns `int`).
- Define a class `Student` that publicly inherits from `Person`. It adds a private member `int m_studentId`.
- `Student`'s constructor takes `std::string_view name`, `int age`, and `int studentId`. It must delegate to `Person`'s constructor for the name and age.
- `Student` has a public const getter `getStudentId()` (returns `int`).
- In `main`, read input, construct a `Student`, and print the three output lines using only the `Student` object and its inherited + own getters.

exercises.title: Vehicle hierarchy with inherited behavior
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a small vehicle hierarchy where a `Vehicle` base class provides shared state and behavior, and two derived classes (`Car` and `Truck`) each add their own specialized member. This reinforces that derived classes inherit base members and functions while extending the class with new data.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `carMake` (no spaces, 1-50 characters), an integer `carYear` (1900 <= carYear <= 2100), and an integer `numDoors` (2 <= numDoors <= 6).
- Line 2: a string `truckMake` (no spaces, 1-50 characters), an integer `truckYear` (1900 <= truckYear <= 2100), and a double `payloadTons` (0.1 <= payloadTons <= 100.0).

## Output

- Line 1: `Car: <carMake> (<carYear>), <numDoors> doors`
- Line 2: `Truck: <truckMake> (<truckYear>), <payloadTons>t payload`

`payloadTons` is printed with exactly one decimal place.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Vehicle` with private members `std::string m_make` and `int m_year`. Its constructor takes `std::string_view make` and `int year`.
- `Vehicle` has public const getters `getMake()` (returns `std::string_view`) and `getYear()` (returns `int`).
- Define a class `Car` that publicly inherits from `Vehicle`. It adds a private member `int m_numDoors`. Its constructor takes `std::string_view make`, `int year`, and `int numDoors`, delegating to `Vehicle`'s constructor.
- `Car` has a public const getter `getNumDoors()` (returns `int`).
- Define a class `Truck` that publicly inherits from `Vehicle`. It adds a private member `double m_payloadTons`. Its constructor takes `std::string_view make`, `int year`, and `double payloadTons`, delegating to `Vehicle`'s constructor.
- `Truck` has a public const getter `getPayloadTons()` (returns `double`).
- In `main`, read input, construct one `Car` and one `Truck`, and print the output using inherited and own getters. Use `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)` for the payload.

test_cases.label: Common vehicles
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Car: Toyota (2022), 4 doors
Truck: Ford (2020), 5.5t payload

test_cases.label: Sports car and heavy truck
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Car: Porsche (2024), 2 doors
Truck: Volvo (2019), 18.0t payload

test_cases.label: Edge values
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Car: Mini (1960), 2 doors
Truck: Mack (2100), 100.0t payload

lessons.title: Order of construction of derived classes

exercises.title: Observe construction and destruction order
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Create a `Base` and `Derived` class hierarchy that prints messages during construction and destruction, demonstrating that C++ constructs the base portion first and destroys it last.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces, 1-50 characters) representing the name of the derived object.

## Output

- Line 1: `Base(<name>) constructed`
- Line 2: `Derived(<name>) constructed`
- Line 3: `Derived(<name>) destroyed`
- Line 4: `Base(<name>) destroyed`

## Requirements

- Define a class `Base` with a protected `std::string` member `m_name`.
- `Base` has a default constructor that prints `Base(<m_name>) constructed` followed by a newline. Initialize `m_name` to `"unnamed"` by default.
- `Base` has a destructor that prints `Base(<m_name>) destroyed` followed by a newline.
- Define a class `Derived` that publicly inherits from `Base`.
- `Derived` has a constructor that takes `std::string_view name` and assigns it to the inherited `m_name`. The constructor then prints `Derived(<m_name>) constructed` followed by a newline.
- `Derived` has a destructor that prints `Derived(<m_name>) destroyed` followed by a newline.
- In `main`, read the name from input, create a `Derived` object in its own scope block, and let it be destroyed when the block ends.

test_cases.label: Basic object lifecycle
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Base(unnamed) constructed
Derived(Widget) constructed
Derived(Widget) destroyed
Base(Widget) destroyed

test_cases.label: Short name
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Base(unnamed) constructed
Derived(X) constructed
Derived(X) destroyed
Base(X) destroyed

test_cases.label: Longer name
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Base(unnamed) constructed
Derived(GameEngine) constructed
Derived(GameEngine) destroyed
Base(GameEngine) destroyed

exercises.title: Multi-level construction order
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Extend the construction-order concept to a three-level hierarchy: `Animal` -> `Pet` -> `Dog`. Each level prints a message during construction and destruction, letting you verify that construction proceeds from the most-base class downward and destruction proceeds from the most-derived class upward.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces, 1-50 characters) for the dog's name.
- Line 2: a string `breed` (no spaces, 1-50 characters) for the dog's breed.

## Output

- Line 1: `Animal constructed`
- Line 2: `Pet(name) constructed`
- Line 3: `Dog(name, breed) constructed`
- Line 4: `Dog(name, breed) destroyed`
- Line 5: `Pet(name) destroyed`
- Line 6: `Animal destroyed`

where `name` and `breed` are the values read from input.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Animal` with a default constructor that prints `Animal constructed` and a destructor that prints `Animal destroyed`.
- Define a class `Pet` that publicly inherits from `Animal`. `Pet` has a protected `std::string m_name` member. Its constructor takes `std::string_view name`, assigns it to `m_name`, and prints `Pet(<m_name>) constructed`. Its destructor prints `Pet(<m_name>) destroyed`.
- Define a class `Dog` that publicly inherits from `Pet`. `Dog` has a private `std::string m_breed` member. Its constructor takes `std::string_view name` and `std::string_view breed`, assigns `breed` to `m_breed`, and prints `Dog(<m_name>, <m_breed>) constructed`. Its destructor prints `Dog(<m_name>, <m_breed>) destroyed`.
- In `main`, read input, create a `Dog` inside a scope block, and let it be destroyed.

test_cases.label: Typical dog
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Animal constructed
Pet(Rex) constructed
Dog(Rex, Shepherd) constructed
Dog(Rex, Shepherd) destroyed
Pet(Rex) destroyed
Animal destroyed

test_cases.label: Short names
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Animal constructed
Pet(Bo) constructed
Dog(Bo, Pug) constructed
Dog(Bo, Pug) destroyed
Pet(Bo) destroyed
Animal destroyed

test_cases.label: Longer names
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Animal constructed
Pet(Maximilian) constructed
Dog(Maximilian, GoldenRetriever) constructed
Dog(Maximilian, GoldenRetriever) destroyed
Pet(Maximilian) destroyed
Animal destroyed

lessons.title: Constructors and initialization of derived classes

exercises.title: Passing Arguments to a Base Constructor
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Create a simple two-level class hierarchy where the derived class passes arguments to the base class constructor through its member initializer list.

## Input

Each line of input contains two values: a string `name` (single word, no spaces) and an integer `speed`.

Input ends at EOF.

## Output

For each line of input, print:

```
name: speed km/h
```

where `name` and `speed` are the values read from that line.

End each line with a newline.

## Requirements

- Create a `Vehicle` base class with a private `int m_speed` member. Its constructor takes an `int` and initializes `m_speed`. Provide a `getSpeed()` const member function.
- Create a `Car` derived class (inheriting from `Vehicle`) with a private `std::string m_name` member. Its constructor takes a `std::string` and an `int`, passing the `int` to `Vehicle`'s constructor via the member initializer list.
- Provide a `Car::print()` const member function that outputs the line in the format shown above.
- Do not directly initialize `m_speed` from `Car` — delegate to the base constructor.

test_cases.label: Zero speed
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Parked: 0 km/h
Bike: 45 km/h
Bus: 60 km/h

exercises.title: Three-Level Constructor Chain
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a three-level inheritance chain (`Creature` -> `Monster` -> `BossMonster`) where each level adds a member and each constructor delegates to its immediate parent.

## Input

Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 10) — the number of boss monsters to create.

Next `N` lines: each contains a string `name` (single word), an integer `health`, and an integer `armor`.

## Output

For each boss monster, print:

```
name [health hp, armor armor]
```

For example, if name is `Dragon`, health is `500`, and armor is `30`, print:

```
Dragon [500 hp, 30 armor]
```

End each line with a newline.

## Requirements

- `Creature` has a private `std::string m_name`. Its constructor takes a `std::string`. Provide `getName()` returning `const std::string&`.
- `Monster` derives from `Creature` and adds a private `int m_health`. Its constructor takes a `std::string` and an `int`, passing the string to `Creature`'s constructor. Provide `getHealth()`.
- `BossMonster` derives from `Monster` and adds a private `int m_armor`. Its constructor takes a `std::string`, an `int`, and an `int`, passing the first two to `Monster`'s constructor. Provide a `print()` const member function.
- Each class must only initialize its own members and delegate upward for inherited ones.

test_cases.label: Two boss monsters
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Dragon [500 hp, 30 armor]
Ogre [200 hp, 10 armor]

test_cases.label: Three bosses
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Golem [1000 hp, 99 armor]
Witch [150 hp, 5 armor]
Demon [666 hp, 42 armor]

lessons.title: Inheritance and access specifiers

exercises.title: Protected member access
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Create a base class `Sensor` with a protected data member and a derived class `LabeledSensor` that reads the protected member directly to produce formatted output.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `label` (1-50 characters, no spaces).
- Line 2: an integer `reading` (-1000 <= reading <= 1000).

## Output

Exactly two lines:
1. `Sensor reading: <reading>`
2. `<label>: <reading> [<status>]`

where `<status>` is `OK` if `reading >= 0`, or `WARN` if `reading < 0`.

## Requirements

- Define a class `Sensor` with a `protected` member `int m_reading` and a `public` constructor taking an `int`.
- `Sensor` has a `public` member function `void printReading() const` that prints `Sensor reading: <m_reading>`.
- Define a class `LabeledSensor` that inherits **publicly** from `Sensor`.
- `LabeledSensor` has a `private` member `std::string m_label` and a constructor taking `const std::string&` and `int`.
- `LabeledSensor` has a `public` member function `void printLabeled() const` that accesses `m_reading` directly (not through a getter) and prints `<label>: <reading> [<status>]`.
- In `main`, read the label and reading, create a `LabeledSensor`, call `printReading()` then `printLabeled()`.

exercises.title: Private inheritance hides the base interface
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Demonstrate how private inheritance restricts access. A `Stack` class privately inherits from a `Container` that tracks a count, then re-exposes only the operations it wants through its own public interface.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 10), the number of push operations.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers (0 <= each <= 1000).

## Output

- Line 1: `Size: <n>`
- Remaining lines: one integer per line, popped in LIFO order (last pushed is first printed).

## Requirements

- Define a class `Container` with a `protected` member `int m_count{}` and two `public` member functions: `int size() const` (returns `m_count`) and `bool empty() const` (returns whether count is 0).
- Define a class `Stack` that inherits **privately** from `Container`.
- `Stack` has a `private` fixed-size array `int m_data[10]{}` for storage.
- `Stack` has three `public` member functions:
  - `void push(int value)`: stores value at index `m_count` and increments `m_count` (accessed directly because it is `protected`).
  - `int pop()`: decrements `m_count` and returns the value at that index.
  - `int size() const`: calls `Container::size()` and returns the result. This re-exposes size through the `Stack` interface, since private inheritance made it inaccessible from outside.
- `Stack` does **not** re-expose `empty()` -- it stays hidden.
- In `main`, read input, push all values, print `Size: <n>`, then pop and print each value on its own line.

lessons.title: Adding new functionality to a derived class

exercises.title: Extending a Vehicle Class
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Given a `Vehicle` base class, create a `Truck` derived class that adds new functionality: a cargo capacity and the ability to load and query cargo.

## Input

- Line 1: a string (the truck's model name, no spaces).
- Line 2: an integer (maximum cargo capacity in kg).
- Line 3: an integer (amount of cargo to load in kg).

## Output

Exactly three lines:
- Line 1: `Model: <model>`
- Line 2: `Capacity: <capacity>`
- Line 3: `Loaded: <amount>` where `<amount>` is the lesser of the requested load and the capacity (do not exceed capacity).

## Requirements

1. A `Vehicle` base class is provided with a `std::string m_model` member and a `getModel()` accessor. Do not modify it.
2. Create a `Truck` class that publicly inherits from `Vehicle`.
3. `Truck` must add a `private` member `int m_capacity{}` and a `private` member `int m_loaded{ 0 }`.
4. `Truck`'s constructor takes a `std::string_view model` and an `int capacity`, forwarding `model` to the `Vehicle` constructor.
5. Add a `public` member function `int getCapacity() const` that returns `m_capacity`.
6. Add a `public` member function `void load(int amount)` that adds `amount` to `m_loaded`, but clamps the total so it never exceeds `m_capacity`.
7. Add a `public` member function `int getLoaded() const` that returns `m_loaded`.
8. In `main`, read input, construct a `Truck`, call `load`, and print the output.

exercises.title: Bank Account with Interest
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Given a `BankAccount` base class that tracks an owner name and balance, create a `SavingsAccount` derived class that adds an interest rate and the ability to apply interest.

## Input

- Line 1: a string (account owner name, no spaces).
- Line 2: an integer (initial balance in cents).
- Line 3: an integer (annual interest rate as a percentage, e.g. 5 means 5%).
- Line 4: an integer `n` (number of times to apply interest, 1 <= n <= 3).

## Output

Exactly two lines:
- Line 1: `Owner: <name>`
- Line 2: `Balance: <balance>` where `<balance>` is the balance after applying interest `n` times (integer arithmetic: each application adds `balance * rate / 100`, using integer division).

## Requirements

1. A `BankAccount` base class is provided. Do not modify it.
2. Create a `SavingsAccount` class that publicly inherits from `BankAccount`.
3. `SavingsAccount` adds a `private` member `int m_rate{}` (the interest percentage).
4. Its constructor takes `std::string_view owner`, `int balance`, and `int rate`, forwarding the first two to the `BankAccount` constructor.
5. Add a `public` method `void applyInterest()` that increases the balance by `getBalance() * m_rate / 100`. Use the inherited `deposit()` method to add the interest amount.
6. Add a `public` method `int getRate() const` returning `m_rate`.
7. In `main`, read input, construct a `SavingsAccount`, apply interest `n` times, and print the output.

lessons.title: Calling inherited functions and redefining behavior

exercises.title: Extending Base Greetings
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Practice redefining a base class function in a derived class and calling the base version explicitly using the `Base::function()` syntax.

## Input

- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces, 1-50 characters).

## Output

- Line 1: `Hello, <name>!` (produced by calling the base class version).
- Line 2: `Welcome aboard, <name>!` (added by the derived class).

## Requirements

- Create a class `Greeter` with a public member function `void greet(const std::string& name)` that prints `Hello, <name>!` followed by a newline.
- Create a class `FriendlyGreeter` that publicly inherits from `Greeter`.
- In `FriendlyGreeter`, redefine `greet` so that it first calls `Greeter::greet(name)` to print the base greeting, then prints `Welcome aboard, <name>!` followed by a newline.
- In `main`, read the name, create a `FriendlyGreeter` object, and call `greet` on it.

exercises.title: Shape Area Reporter
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Build a two-level class hierarchy where the derived class redefines a `report` function to include additional information while reusing the base class logic via explicit `Base::function()` calls.

## Input

- Line 1: two space-separated doubles `width` and `height` (0.1 <= each <= 1000.0) representing the dimensions of a rectangle.

## Output

- Line 1: `Shape area: <area>` where `<area>` is width * height, printed with exactly 1 decimal place.
- Line 2: `Rectangle <width> x <height>` where width and height are printed with exactly 1 decimal place.

## Requirements

- Create a class `Shape` with:
  - Protected member variables `double m_width` and `double m_height`.
  - A public constructor that takes `double width` and `double height`.
  - A public member function `double area() const` that returns `m_width * m_height`.
  - A public member function `void report() const` that prints `Shape area: ` followed by the result of `area()` with 1 decimal place, then a newline.
- Create a class `Rectangle` that publicly inherits from `Shape`.
  - Its constructor takes `double width` and `double height` and forwards them to `Shape`.
  - It redefines `report() const` to first call `Shape::report()`, then print `Rectangle <m_width> x <m_height>` (each with 1 decimal place) followed by a newline.
- In `main`, read width and height, create a `Rectangle`, and call `report()` on it.
- Use `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)` from `<iomanip>` for formatting.

lessons.title: Hiding inherited functionality

exercises.title: Restricting access with using declarations
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Given a `Base` class with two public member functions (`getValue` and `setValue`), create a `ReadOnly` derived class that hides `setValue` by moving it to the private section with a `using` declaration. The class should still be able to use `setValue` internally through a controlled `reset` function.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `initial` and `resetVal` (-10000 <= each <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: `initial: <initial>` -- the value after construction.
- Line 2: `reset: <resetVal>` -- the value after calling `reset(resetVal)`.
- Line 3: `base ref: <resetVal>` -- the value accessed through a `Base&` reference (demonstrating that hiding only works through the derived type).

## Requirements

- A `Base` class is provided with `int getValue() const` and `void setValue(int v)` as public members, and `int m_val{}` as a protected member.
- Define a class `ReadOnly` that publicly inherits from `Base`.
- Inside `ReadOnly`'s `private` section, add `using Base::setValue;` to hide `setValue` from external callers.
- Add a public constructor `ReadOnly(int v)` that calls `setValue(v)` internally to set the initial value.
- Add a public member function `void reset(int v)` that calls `setValue(v)` internally.
- In `main`, construct a `ReadOnly` object with `initial`, print its value, call `reset(resetVal)`, print again, then create a `Base&` reference to the object and call `getValue()` through it.

exercises.title: Deleting inherited functions in a FixedArray
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Given a `DynamicArray` base class that supports `push_back`, `size`, `get`, and `resize`, create a `FixedArray` derived class that deletes `resize` and `push_back` so that the array size cannot change after construction. The `FixedArray` should fill all elements from input at construction time.

## Input

- Line 1: an integer `n` (1 <= n <= 20), the fixed array size.
- Line 2: `n` space-separated integers (-10000 <= each <= 10000).

## Output

- Line 1: `size: <n>`
- Line 2: the elements printed space-separated.
- Line 3: `fixed: yes`

## Requirements

- A `DynamicArray` class is provided with `push_back`, `resize`, `size`, and `get` public member functions, plus a constructor taking an initial capacity.
- Define `FixedArray` that publicly inherits from `DynamicArray`.
- `FixedArray` constructor takes `const int* data` and `int count`, passes `count` to the `DynamicArray` constructor, then calls `push_back` internally for each element.
- Delete `resize` in `FixedArray`: `void resize(int) = delete;`
- Delete `push_back` in `FixedArray`: `void push_back(int) = delete;`
- In `main`, read `n` values into a local array, construct a `FixedArray`, print its size and elements, then print `fixed: yes`.

lessons.title: Multiple inheritance

exercises.title: Multi-base disambiguation
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Create a class `FlyingFish` that inherits from both `Bird` and `Fish`, then call methods from each base class using explicit disambiguation.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `wingSpan` and `depth` (1 <= each <= 1000), representing the bird's wingspan and the fish's max depth.

## Output

- Line 1: `wings: <wingSpan>`
- Line 2: `depth: <depth>`
- Line 3: `name: FlyingFish`

## Requirements

- Define a class `Bird` with a protected `int m_wingSpan{}` member, a constructor taking an `int` that sets it, a public `void showWings() const` that prints `wings: <m_wingSpan>` followed by a newline, and a public `std::string name() const` that returns `"Bird"`.
- Define a class `Fish` with a protected `int m_depth{}` member, a constructor taking an `int` that sets it, a public `void showDepth() const` that prints `depth: <m_depth>` followed by a newline, and a public `std::string name() const` that returns `"Fish"`.
- Define a class `FlyingFish` that publicly inherits from both `Bird` and `Fish`. Its constructor takes `int wingSpan` and `int depth` and passes them to the respective base constructors.
- `FlyingFish` has its own `std::string name() const` that returns `"FlyingFish"`.
- In `main`, create a `FlyingFish`, call `showWings()`, `showDepth()`, and print `name: ` followed by the result of calling the `FlyingFish` version of `name()`.

exercises.title: Diamond ambiguity resolver
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal

Experience the diamond problem firsthand: class `A` holds a value, `B` and `C` each inherit from `A`, and `D` inherits from both `B` and `C`. Resolve the resulting ambiguity by using `Base::` qualification to access each copy of `A`'s data independently.

## Input

- Line 1: two integers `bVal` and `cVal` (-10000 <= each <= 10000), values to store in the `A` subobject that came through `B` and the `A` subobject that came through `C`, respectively.

## Output

- Line 1: `B::value: <bVal>`
- Line 2: `C::value: <cVal>`
- Line 3: `sum: <bVal + cVal>`

## Requirements

- Define a class `A` with a public `int m_value{}` member and a constructor taking an `int` that sets it.
- Define a class `B` that publicly inherits from `A`. Its constructor takes an `int` and forwards it to `A`.
- Define a class `C` that publicly inherits from `A`. Its constructor takes an `int` and forwards it to `A`.
- Define a class `D` that publicly inherits from both `B` and `C`. Its constructor takes two ints (`bVal`, `cVal`) and passes them to `B` and `C` respectively.
- In `D`, write a public method `void show() const` that:
  - Prints `B::value: ` followed by `B::m_value` and a newline.
  - Prints `C::value: ` followed by `C::m_value` and a newline.
  - Prints `sum: ` followed by `B::m_value + C::m_value` and a newline.
- In `main`, read the two values, construct a `D`, and call `show()`.

lessons.title: Chapter 24 summary and quiz
lessons.summary_md:
## Chapter 24 Summary

This chapter covered inheritance, the mechanism that lets one class reuse and extend another class's members without duplicating code.

### Introduction and basic mechanics (24.1, 24.2)

Inheritance models an "is-a" relationship: a `Dog` is an `Animal`, a `Car` is a `Vehicle`. You write `class Derived : public Base` to make `Derived` inherit all non-private members of `Base`. The derived class gets the base class's public and protected member variables and member functions automatically. What is **not** inherited: constructors, destructors, and assignment operators.

### Construction and destruction order (24.3)

When a derived object is created, the base portion is constructed first, then the derived portion. Destruction happens in reverse: derived destructor runs first, then the base destructor. This guarantees that inherited members are fully initialized before the derived constructor body executes, and still intact when the derived destructor runs.

### Constructors and initialization (24.4)

Because constructors are not inherited, the derived class must explicitly call a base constructor in its member initializer list. If you omit this call, the compiler attempts to use the base class's default constructor. If no default constructor exists, compilation fails.

### Access specifiers and inheritance (24.5)

The type of inheritance (public, protected, private) controls how base members are exposed through the derived class. With public inheritance, public base members stay public and protected members stay protected. With protected inheritance, both become protected in the derived class. With private inheritance, both become private. In practice, public inheritance is used almost exclusively.

### Adding and redefining functionality (24.6, 24.7)

Derived classes can add entirely new member functions and member variables. They can also redefine inherited functions by defining a function with the same name and signature. Inside the redefined function, you can call the base version explicitly using `Base::functionName()` to extend rather than completely replace the inherited behavior.

### Hiding inherited functionality (24.8)

You can hide a base class function in the derived class by marking it `= delete` or by changing its access level using a `using` declaration. A `using Base::functionName` declaration in a different access section of the derived class changes the visibility of that function. Marking an inherited function `= delete` prevents it from being called through the derived class entirely.

### Multiple inheritance (24.9)

C++ allows a class to inherit from more than one base class: `class Derived : public Base1, public Base2`. This introduces the diamond problem: if two base classes share a common ancestor, the derived class ends up with two copies of the ancestor's members, creating ambiguity. While C++ provides solutions (such as explicit scope resolution), multiple inheritance adds significant complexity and should be used sparingly.

exercises.title: Employee Hierarchy with Construction Order and Redefinitions
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Build a three-level class hierarchy (`Person` -> `Employee` -> `Manager`) that demonstrates construction/destruction order, calling base class constructors, adding new functionality, and redefining inherited functions.

## Input
- Line 1: a string `name` (no spaces), an integer `age`, and a string `department` (no spaces).
- Line 2: an integer `teamSize`.

## Output
First, print the construction trace (one line per constructor, in the order they execute):
```
Person constructed: <name>, age <age>
Employee constructed: <department>
Manager constructed: team of <teamSize>
```
Then print a blank line, followed by the manager's description produced by calling `describe()` on the `Manager` object:
```
<name> (age <age>), <department> dept, manages <teamSize>
```
Then print a blank line, followed by the destruction trace (reverse order):
```
Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
Person destroyed
```

## Requirements
1. `Person` has `std::string m_name` and `int m_age` (both private). Constructor takes `std::string_view name` and `int age`, prints the construction message. Destructor prints `Person destroyed`. Public `describe() const` returns a `std::string`: `"<name> (age <age>)"`.
2. `Employee` publicly inherits from `Person`. Adds private `std::string m_department`. Constructor takes `std::string_view name`, `int age`, and `std::string_view department`; delegates to `Person`'s constructor and prints `Employee constructed: <department>`. Destructor prints `Employee destroyed`. Redefines `describe() const` to return `"<base describe>, <department> dept"` by calling `Person::describe()`.
3. `Manager` publicly inherits from `Employee`. Adds private `int m_teamSize`. Constructor takes all four parameters; delegates to `Employee`'s constructor and prints `Manager constructed: team of <teamSize>`. Destructor prints `Manager destroyed`. Redefines `describe() const` to return `"<Employee describe>, manages <teamSize>"` by calling `Employee::describe()`.
4. In `main`, create a `Manager` inside a scope block. Between construction and destruction, print a blank line, call `describe()`, then print another blank line before the block ends.
5. You may include `<iostream>`, `<string>`, `<string_view>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- typical manager
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person constructed: Alice, age 35
Employee constructed: Engineering
Manager constructed: team of 8

Alice (age 35), Engineering dept, manages 8

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
Person destroyed

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single-person team
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person constructed: Bob, age 28
Employee constructed: Sales
Manager constructed: team of 1

Bob (age 28), Sales dept, manages 1

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
Person destroyed

test_cases.label: Hidden -- large team older manager
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Person constructed: Zara, age 60
Employee constructed: Research
Manager constructed: team of 50

Zara (age 60), Research dept, manages 50

Manager destroyed
Employee destroyed
Person destroyed

exercises.title: Protected Access and Hiding Inherited Functions
exercises.prompt_md:
## Goal
Build a `Vehicle` base class and a `RaceCarDriver` class hierarchy that exercises protected members, adding new functionality, calling base class functions explicitly, and hiding an inherited function.

## Input
- Line 1: a string `model` (no spaces), an integer `topSpeed`, and an integer `currentSpeed`.
- Line 2: an integer `boostCount` (number of times to apply boost).

## Output
Print the initial status, then apply `boostCount` boosts (each adds 30 to speed, capped at `topSpeed`), printing the speed after each boost. Finally print the status again. The `RaceCar` class hides the base `setSpeed()` function to prevent arbitrary speed changes.

Format:
```
<model>: <currentSpeed>/<topSpeed> km/h
Boost 1: <speed> km/h
Boost 2: <speed> km/h
...
<model>: <finalSpeed>/<topSpeed> km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar
```
The last line is printed by attempting to call `setSpeed(0)` on the `RaceCar`, which outputs the disabled message instead of changing speed.

## Requirements
1. `Vehicle` class with protected `std::string m_model`, `int m_topSpeed`, and `int m_currentSpeed`. Constructor takes all three. Public `status() const` prints `"<model>: <currentSpeed>/<topSpeed> km/h\n"`. Public `setSpeed(int speed)` sets `m_currentSpeed` to `speed`.
2. `RaceCar` publicly inherits from `Vehicle`. Constructor delegates to `Vehicle`. Adds public `void boost()` that increases `m_currentSpeed` by 30, capping at `m_topSpeed` using the protected members directly. Hides `setSpeed` by redefining it: `RaceCar` declares its own `setSpeed(int)` that prints `"setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar\n"` and does not change speed. Also adds a public `int getSpeed() const` that returns `m_currentSpeed`.
3. In `main`, create a `RaceCar`, print status, apply boosts printing each result using `getSpeed()`, print status, then call `setSpeed(0)`.
4. You may include `<iostream>`, `<string>`, `<string_view>`, `<algorithm>`.

test_cases.label: Sample -- three boosts with cap
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Ferrari: 150/300 km/h
Boost 1: 180 km/h
Boost 2: 210 km/h
Boost 3: 240 km/h
Ferrari: 240/300 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar

test_cases.label: Hidden -- boost hits top speed cap
test_cases.expected_stdout:
GoKart: 50/80 km/h
Boost 1: 80 km/h
Boost 2: 80 km/h
GoKart: 80/80 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar

test_cases.label: Hidden -- zero boosts
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sedan: 0/200 km/h
Sedan: 0/200 km/h
setSpeed is disabled for RaceCar
